title: Quiz: Classical Control Systems for QEC

questions.question: Why are FPGAs commonly used for QEC decoding?

options.text: They are cheaper than other options
They provide deterministic, low-latency processing suitable for real-time feedback

questions.explanation: FPGAs provide deterministic timing and sub-microsecond latency, essential for real-time QEC feedback. They can process syndromes in parallel and provide the consistent, low-latency response needed for superconducting QEC cycles.

questions.question: What is the typical latency budget for a complete QEC cycle on superconducting qubits?
questions.explanation: Superconducting QEC cycles typically need to complete in about 1 microsecond (1000 ns). This includes measurement (~400 ns), data transfer (~100 ns), decoding (~400 ns), and correction application (~100 ns).

questions.question: What is Pauli frame tracking and why is it used?

options.text: Tracking which Pauli gates have been applied to initialize the system
Software-based tracking of corrections that can be applied classically instead of physically
Monitoring the frame rate of quantum operations
Tracking errors in the Pauli basis

questions.explanation: Pauli frame tracking keeps a classical record of pending Pauli corrections. Since Pauli operators commute through Clifford gates (with known transformations), we can track them in software rather than applying physical gates, saving time and reducing errors.

questions.question: What is a major limitation of lookup table decoders?

options.text: They are too slow for real-time use
Memory requirements grow exponentially with code size

questions.explanation: Lookup table decoders require storing all possible syndrome-to-correction mappings. The number of syndromes grows exponentially with code size (2^num_syndromes), making this approach impractical for large codes beyond about distance 5-7.

questions.question: Why is Union-Find preferred for large-scale FPGA decoding?

options.text: It has the lowest error rate
It has near-linear complexity and is highly parallelizable

questions.explanation: Union-Find has near-linear time complexity O(d² α(d²)) and is naturally parallelizable, making it well-suited for FPGA implementation. It scales efficiently to large code distances (d=20+) while maintaining reasonable latency.

questions.question: In a hierarchical control system, what is handled at the local FPGA level?

options.text: Syndrome decoding and correction for individual logical qubits

questions.explanation: Local FPGAs handle time-critical operations for individual logical qubits: syndrome extraction, decoding, and correction tracking. Higher-level coordination (algorithm scheduling, inter-logical operations) is handled by global controllers with relaxed timing constraints.

questions.question: Pauli frame tracking can completely replace physical correction gates for all quantum circuits.
questions.explanation: False. Pauli frame tracking works for Clifford circuits, but non-Clifford gates (like T gates) don't commute nicely with Pauli operators. Before applying a T gate, the accumulated Pauli frame must be 'cashed out' by applying physical correction gates.
